title: Why Newmont Stock Bumped Higher Today
author: Eric Volkman
description: Newmont stock rose 2.5% on Tuesday following the announcement of three new C-suite executives effective July 1, 2026. The new CFO is Brian Tabolt, COO is Mark Rodgers, and CTO is David Thornton—all internal promotions. The company stated these appointments strengthen its ability to execute strategy with clarity and focus, positioning it to improve performance and deliver shareholder value.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Stock gained 2.5% on announcement of three experienced internal promotions to key C-suite positions. The company's ability to fill leadership roles from within its own workforce demonstrates organizational strength and continuity, with management expressing confidence in executing strategy and delivering long-term shareholder value.
Mentioned only as prior employer of incoming CFO Brian Tabolt; no direct impact on the company from this news.
Mentioned only as prior employer of incoming CFO Brian Tabolt; no direct impact on the company from this news.

title: Gold Miners, Nuclear, Homebuilders: 3 Trades That Could Bounce After Trump's Hormuz Deal
author: Piero Cingari
description: Following Trump's announcement of a deal with Iran that reopens the Strait of Hormuz, crude oil fell to $80/barrel. However, three sectors remain significantly underperforming since the February conflict began: gold miners (down 17-18%), nuclear/uranium companies (down 15%), and homebuilders (down 9.7%). These sectors were hit by dual pressures—lower commodity prices and higher interest rates driven by inflation concerns—and may recover if the ceasefire holds and yields decline.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Down 17% from pre-war levels due to falling gold prices (down 17%) and elevated energy costs. However, showed some recovery (+1.42%) following the Iran deal announcement.
Down 9.7% since conflict began as higher Treasury yields pushed mortgage rates higher, cooling buyer demand. Showed modest recovery (+1.73%) on deal announcement.
Down 15.01%, the second-worst performer, as rising yields reduced present value of future cash flows for nuclear companies. Declined 0.65% despite broader market recovery.
World's largest gold miner remains 18% below pre-war levels due to falling gold prices and higher energy costs. Rallied 5.6% and 1.93% on deal news but still significantly underperforming.
Up 10% since February conflict began, reaching record highs. Slight decline of 0.25% on this specific day but maintains strong overall performance.

title: Gold Miners or Silver Miners: Which Precious Metals ETF Is the Better Buy Right Now?
author: Sara Appino
description: The article compares two precious metals mining ETFs: Sprott Gold Miners ETF (SGDM), which focuses on North American gold producers with a lower 0.46% expense ratio, and Global X Silver Miners ETF (SIL), which provides global silver exposure with higher 1-year returns (83% vs 53%) but greater volatility. Gold miners are recommended for new precious metals investors, while silver miners suit those seeking higher-conviction bets on industrial and monetary demand convergence.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Lower expense ratio (0.46%), more stable with lower beta (0.53) and maximum drawdown (45%), better 5-year growth ($2,214 from $1,000), and recommended as the more established entry point for new precious metals investors.

Higher 1-year returns (83%), higher dividend yield (1.10%), and positioned as a higher-conviction bet for investors with existing precious metals exposure seeking exposure to industrial demand and green transition themes.
Listed as a top holding (8.48%) in SGDM with no specific performance commentary provided in the article.
Listed as the largest holding (21.52%) in SIL with no specific performance commentary provided in the article.
Listed as a top holding (12.66%) in SIL with no specific performance commentary provided in the article.
Listed as a top holding (11.04%) in SIL with no specific performance commentary provided in the article.
Listed as a top holding (10.46%) in SGDM with no specific performance commentary provided in the article.

title: The Hormuz Reopening Trade: These 20 Large-Cap Stocks Still Haven't Caught Up To Pre-War Levels
author: Piero Cingari
description: Following President Trump's announcement of a U.S.-Iran peace deal and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, oil prices plunged 5.4% to $80/barrel. However, 20 large-cap stocks worth over $100 billion remain trading 15-24% below their pre-war levels from February 27, 2026. The laggards span consumer staples, healthcare, software, and mining sectors, with weakness extending beyond the war premium as these companies face ongoing margin pressures from higher energy costs.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Down 24% from pre-war levels, the worst performer on the screen, indicating significant underperformance despite market rally
Trading below February 27 pre-war close by 15-24%, consumer staple facing margin pressures from elevated energy costs
Among the lagging consumer anchors still trading below pre-war levels despite Hormuz reopening
Consumer staple anchor trading below pre-war levels, affected by ongoing cost pressures
China's largest internet company hit hardest by higher energy costs as China is world's biggest crude importer; among worst performers
Chinese internet name near bottom of laggards, impacted by elevated energy costs affecting Chinese companies
Software sector laggard trading below pre-war levels despite market rally
Software and IT services company among the screened laggards below pre-war close
IT services company screening among laggards, trading below pre-war levels
Mining sector laggard trading below pre-war levels despite gold trade benefiting from geopolitical uncertainty

title: Gold Just Tumbled Into its First Bear Market Since 2022. Here's What Investors Need to Know.
author: Neha Chamaria
description: Gold has entered bear market territory, declining over 25% from its January 2026 all-time high of $5,608.35 per ounce. The sell-off is driven by sticky inflation forcing the Federal Reserve to delay rate cuts, causing investors to pivot toward yield-bearing Treasury bonds over non-interest-bearing assets like gold. While gold miners face near-term headwinds from lower projected production and falling prices, the correction presents a buying opportunity for long-term bullish investors.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Strong balance sheet and free cash flows from the gold rally, but facing headwinds from lower projected 2026 gold production combined with falling gold prices. Stock performance reflects near-term challenges despite solid fundamentals.

Similar to Newmont, benefits from strong balance sheets and cash generation, but projected lower gold production for 2026 amid falling prices creates near-term pressure. Fundamentals remain solid despite volatility.

Recommended as the largest gold mining ETF with diversified exposure to 60 global mining companies, mitigating single-stock risks. Suitable for investors seeking gold sector exposure while managing volatility.
